chunks.title: Kale Nutritional Claims
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses common nutritional claims about kale, questioning its benefits such as β-carotene and vitamin K. They suggest that alternatives like liver and egg yolks provide better nutrition.
chunks.transcript: on to kale bashing this is one of my favorite things because who doesn't love a little good confirmation bias no but honestly i try not to be biased in this way i just find it interesting when mainstream reporting here at insider magazine from 2019 says kale might be bad for you in some cases here's when you should be careful about eating it how about all the time insider magazine but uh this just looks gross to me i would never put my hands in a bucket of kale like that kale is packed with full of things that are good for you like protein that's a joke vitamin a that's pretty much a joke why would you get beta-carotene from kale when you can get it from liver or egg yolks and vitamin k well kale only has vitamin k1 and we know that vitamin k2 is critical

chunks.title: Thyroid Interference
chunks.summary: The video highlights how isothiocyanates in kale can affect iodine uptake and potentially harm thyroid function. It references studies indicating that excessive kale consumption may lead to health issues.
chunks.transcript: vitamin k1 has no effects on cardiovascular risk and things like the rotterdam study but most people in the popular press and most vegans can't understand that vitamin k1 and k2 are different things neither could the doctors on that show when i went on there they kept yelling at me you can't get vitamin k and animal foods and i was just like scratching my head what what are you actually freaking talking about insider says but if you have an underactive thyroid it might be a good idea to not eat too much of it no this is what i've talked about so many times the isothiocyanates in kale can affect iodine uptake at the level of the thyroid i think these are harming our thyroids and there's no reason to kill certain compounds and vegetables can interfere with thyroid hormone synthesis and essentially block the iodine yup yup i've talked about that you probably would have to eat an excessive amount for this to happen though well i mean when i was a vegan i ate two heads of kale a day i'm pretty sure i was nuking my thyroid and as i've shown in previous podcasts there are studies that suggest that goitrin is another isothiocyanate found in things like brussels sprouts or chard and in levels that most people consume in one salad or easily on a daily basis they can affect radioactive iodine

chunks.title: Pesticide Contamination
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to kale's high contamination levels with pesticides, particularly focusing on a pesticide named dacthal and its detrimental effects on the thyroid. The speaker emphasizes the importance of washing kale thoroughly before consumption.
chunks.transcript: this one is great kale also lists highly in the u.s for being contaminated with pesticides so you should wash it thoroughly before eating it raw well the main pesticide that kale is contaminated with is called dakthal kale is now one of the most pesticide contaminated vegetables in 2019 and if you think you can get organic kale and get out of this i have news for you stay tuned in this podcast kale samples contain traces of a weed killer named dakthal and dcpa or dacthal is actually acknowledged by the epa the environmental protection needs agency of the united states to have negative endocrine disrupting effects on the thyroid the epa says glyphosate is totally fine but dakthal does affect the thyroid negatively so if you're getting non-organic kale you're getting a double whammy to your thyroid lest you think you can avoid this by getting organic kale stay tuned

chunks.title: Heavy Metals in Kale
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces alarming findings regarding thallium contamination in kale, linking it to health issues experienced by individuals consuming large amounts of the vegetable. They highlight research supporting these claims.
chunks.transcript: so the list of reasons you don't want to eat kale and leafy greens grows this is from the highly reputable source delish obviously i'm joking in 2018 but they did report that people are getting seriously sick from eating kale it wasn't from the dakthal it was from the thallium this is a really interesting study this is a really interesting story i want to try and get ernie hubbard on the podcast and if you look at these articles from craftsmanship quarterly which is the best storytelling i can find about this vegetable detective this is fascinating ernie hubbard is a practitioner who worked in marin county and what he began to see in his clinic the preventive medical center of marin was that a number of people in this growing health trend over the last 12 years 2010 um were having problems non-specific symptoms chronic fatigue issues like that and they were eating a lot of kale and eventually he connected the dots and began to realize that yes leafy greens like kale obviously i skipped over a ton of the story in between there guys you can read the whole thing if you want here on the uh on the article but he realized that many of these leafy greens do in fact take up heavy metals from the soil we've known this for a long time and kale is one of the worst offenders here many samples of kale have massive levels of thallium in them a a metal that is essentially toxic for humans i think

chunks.title: Anecdotal Evidence of Toxicity
chunks.summary: Personal anecdotes are shared about individuals detoxifying from thallium after consuming kale, indicating potential toxicity. The speaker reflects on their own past experiences with kale consumption.

chunks.title: Validating Scientific Claims
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses ongoing scrutiny of the scientific claims made in the context of kale and thallium contamination. They indicate a desire to follow up on research studies to explore these findings further.
chunks.transcript: uh historically like i said i will do glyphosate levels in the future with regard to the story of ernie hubbard and kale and thallium there's a follow-up article in the craftsman called the vegetable detective part two you can see here they say the first question involved the quality of hubbard science perhaps the simplest answer to this question was delivered by joshua nachman a nutritionist at jhu at johns hopkins university integrative medicine digestive center nachman had occasion to quiz hubbard about his work in response to a query from the huffington post after several exchanges nachman came to the following conclusion what he has found is good science knockman told me it's preliminary not you're ready to be extrapolated the population at large but the chemistry is chemistry it's all factual so that's the most that i could find i want to follow up on the story on ernie hubbard and thalium and kale but if you eat a lot of kale or you have eaten a lot of kale you might want to get your thallium levels checked you can do that through doctor's data work with a functional medicine partitioner or
